1:
"And Abimelech the son of Jerubbaal went to Shechem unto his mother's brethren, and communed with them, and with all the family of the house of his mother's father, saying," The very brief verse notes Abimelech going to Shechem to scheme with his mother's kindred. The grammatical voices note the plot. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Abimelech the son of Jerubbaal went to Shechem unto his mother's brethren, and communed with them" — Abimelech, Gideon's son by a concubine, went to plot with his mother's kin; the and Abimelech the son of Jerubbaal went to Shechem unto his mother's brethren (Abimelech to Shechem); the and communed with them, and with all the family of the house of his mother's father, saying (the plot begun); the figure (the ambitious son scheming with his kindred for the kingdom; the truth that Abimelech sought the throne by the help of his mother's people at Shechem, Judges 8:31; the figure of the ambition that schemes for power, the kindred enlisted; the plot of Abimelech; the conspiracy begun, vv.31). The verse holds together the plot.

On "And Abimelech the son of Jerubbaal went to Shechem unto his mother's brethren," the witnesses note the going. Gill notes Abimelech going to his mother's kin (the plot, Judges 8:31) — the going (to Shechem).

On "and communed with them, and with all the family of the house of his mother's father, saying," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the scheming: "communed with them, and with all the family of the house of his mother's father" — Abimelech scheming with the kindred (the plot); the figure (the ambition that schemes for power); the providence (the plot, the appeal to the men of Shechem to follow, vv.2). The verse displays Abimelech scheming with his mother's kindred.

The verse displays his appeal to the men of Shechem to choose him over the seventy (vv.2).
2:
"Speak, I pray you, in the ears of all the men of Shechem, Whether is better for you, either that all the sons of Jerubbaal, which are threescore and ten persons, reign over you, or that one reign over you? remember also that I am your bone and your flesh." The very brief verse notes Abimelech's appeal to the men of Shechem, pleading his kinship. The grammatical voices note the appeal. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Whether is better for you, either that all the sons of Jerubbaal... reign over you, or that one reign over you? remember also that I am your bone and your flesh" — Abimelech urged the Shechemites to prefer him, their kinsman, to the seventy; the speak, I pray you, in the ears of all the men of Shechem, Whether is better for you, either that all the sons of Jerubbaal, which are threescore and ten persons, reign over you, or that one reign over you? (the false alternative); the remember also that I am your bone and your flesh (the plea of kinship); the figure (the demagogue's appeal, the false choice and the plea of blood; the truth that Abimelech set a false alternative and pleaded kinship to win the throne, Genesis 29:14; the figure of the ambition that flatters and deceives the people to gain power; the appeal of Abimelech; the plea of kinship, vv.1). The verse holds together the appeal.

The verse displays Abimelech's appeal pleading his kinship.

3:
"And his mother's brethren spake of him in the ears of all the men of Shechem all these words: and their hearts inclined to follow Abimelech; for they said, He is our brother." The verse holds together the inclining.

4:
"And they gave him threescore and ten pieces of silver out of the house of Baalberith, wherewith Abimelech hired vain and light persons, which followed him." The very brief verse notes Abimelech hiring vain men with silver from Baal's temple. The grammatical voices note the hire. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "they gave him threescore and ten pieces of silver out of the house of Baalberith, wherewith Abimelech hired vain and light persons, which followed him" — Abimelech hired worthless men with money from the idol's temple; the and they gave him threescore and ten pieces of silver out of the house of Baalberith (silver from Baal's house); the wherewith Abimelech hired vain and light persons, which followed him (vain men hired); the figure (the idol's money buying worthless men for the bloody work; the truth that the wages of the idol-temple bought a band of reckless men, the means as base as the end, Judges 8:33; the figure of the ungodly enterprise furnished by ungodly means, the worthless hired for evil; the hire of Abimelech; the band of vain men, vv.3). The verse holds together the hire.

5:
"And he went unto his father's house at Ophrah, and slew his brethren the sons of Jerubbaal, being threescore and ten persons, upon one stone: notwithstanding yet Jotham the youngest son of Jerubbaal was left; for he hid himself." The witnesses note Abimelech slaying his seventy brethren upon one stone, with only Jotham escaping — the crux of the murderous cruelty of unbridled ambition, that climbs to power over the blood of kindred. On "slew his brethren the sons of Jerubbaal, being threescore and ten persons, upon one stone," interpreters expound the deed. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the bloodshed of ambition — "and he went unto his father's house at Ophrah, and slew his brethren the sons of Jerubbaal, being threescore and ten persons, upon one stone: notwithstanding yet Jotham the youngest son of Jerubbaal was left": Abimelech butchered his seventy brothers on a single stone to clear his path to the throne; the witnesses note the significance — the horror of an ambition that stops at nothing, the murder of seventy brethren, the sons of the deliverer Gideon, slaughtered like victims on one stone to secure a usurped crown, the basest treachery and cruelty in the pursuit of power (the seventy slain by the hired band, the deliberate butchery on one stone, the deepest ingratitude against Gideon's house and the basest crime against kindred); the lesson — that ambition unrestrained by the fear of God becomes murderous, trampling every bond of nature and blood to grasp at power, "they that will be rich fall into... many foolish and hurtful lusts," 1 Timothy 6:9-10; the truth that the love of power, like the love of money, is a root of all evil, leading men to the foulest crimes, "from whence come wars and fightings... ye lust, and have not: ye kill," James 4:1-2; the figure — the cruelty of the wicked who shed innocent blood to advance themselves, the type of every tyrant and of the spirit that murdered the heirs to seize the inheritance, "this is the heir; come, let us kill him," Matthew 21:38; the truth that God marks such blood and will require it, the cry of the seventy not unheard; the figure of the murderous cruelty of unbridled ambition, that climbs to power over the blood of kindred; the call to fear the God who avenges innocent blood; the warning against the ambition that destroys all bonds to grasp at power. The figure of the murderous cruelty of unbridled ambition, that climbs to power over the blood of kindred.

On "and he went unto his father's house at Ophrah, and slew his brethren the sons of Jerubbaal, being threescore and ten persons, upon one stone," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the slaughter. Keil and Ellicott note Abimelech butchering his seventy brothers on one stone to clear his path to the throne, the deliberate murder of Gideon's house (the bloodshed of ambition, 1 Timothy 6:9-10) — the slaughter (seventy on one stone).

On "notwithstanding yet Jotham the youngest son of Jerubbaal was left; for he hid himself," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the one spared. Calvin and Gill note Jotham alone escaping by hiding, God preserving one to pronounce the curse, the love of power leading to the foulest crime yet not utterly prevailing (the lust that kills, James 4:1-2; Matthew 21:38) — the figure (the cruelty of the wicked who shed innocent blood to advance themselves); the providence (the murder, Abimelech made king at Shechem to follow, vv.6). The verse displays Abimelech slaying the seventy, only Jotham left.

The verse displays the blood: crying. The verse displays the men of Shechem making Abimelech king (vv.6).

The verse holds together the crowning.

7:

8:
"The trees went forth on a time to anoint a king over them; and they said unto the olive tree, Reign thou over us." The witnesses note Jotham's parable of the trees going forth to anoint a king — the crux of the oldest of parables, the folly of seeking a ruler and the truth that the worthy decline what the worthless grasp. On "the trees went forth on a time to anoint a king over them; and they said unto the olive tree, Reign thou over us," interpreters expound the fable. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the parable's design — "the trees went forth on a time to anoint a king over them; and they said unto the olive tree, Reign thou over us": Jotham frames a fable in which the trees seek a king, first asking the olive; the witnesses note the significance — the most ancient of parables, framed to expose the folly of the Shechemites in making Abimelech king, the trees a figure of the people seeking a ruler, the worthy and useful trees (the olive, fig, and vine) one by one declining the honour, while only the worthless bramble accepts (the fable holding up a mirror to Shechem, the people's restless desire for a king, the better men unwilling to leave their useful callings for empty rule, the basest grasping at the power the best refuse); the lesson — that those most fit to rule are often least eager for it, content in their fruitful service, while the ambitious and worthless press forward, "seekest thou great things for thyself? seek them not," Jeremiah 45:5; the truth that true greatness lies in fruitful service rather than in lordship, and that the desire to rule is no mark of worth, "whosoever will be great among you, let him be your minister," Matthew 20:26; the figure — the wisdom that exposes the vanity of ambition and the folly of a people who exalt the unworthy, the parable a glass for every generation that prefers the bramble to the olive; the truth that God's true servants seek not their own exaltation but their appointed fruitfulness; the figure of the folly of seeking a ruler and the truth that the worthy decline what the worthless grasp; the call to be content in fruitful service rather than to grasp at power; the warning that a people who crown the bramble reap its fire. The figure of the folly of seeking a ruler, the worthy declining what the worthless grasp.

On "the trees went forth on a time to anoint a king over them," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the parable's frame. Keil and Ellicott note Jotham framing a fable of the trees seeking a king, the oldest of parables, a mirror held up to the folly of Shechem (the parable's design, Jeremiah 45:5) — the fable (the trees seek a king).

On "and they said unto the olive tree, Reign thou over us," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the worthy asked first. The verse displays the trees going forth to anoint a king, asking the olive.

The verse displays the olive declining, unwilling to leave its fatness (vv.9).
9:
"But the olive tree said unto them, Should I leave my fatness, wherewith by me they honour God and man, and go to be promoted over the trees?" The very brief verse notes the olive declining to reign, unwilling to leave its fatness. The grammatical voices note the refusal. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the olive tree said unto them, Should I leave my fatness, wherewith by me they honour God and man, and go to be promoted over the trees?" — the olive refused, unwilling to leave its useful fatness for rule; the but the olive tree said unto them, Should I leave my fatness (the olive's refusal); the wherewith by me they honour God and man, and go to be promoted over the trees? (its useful service); the figure (the olive content with its fruitful service, declining the empty honour of rule; the truth that the olive, useful to God and man, would not forsake its fatness for lordship, Psalm 104:15; the figure of the worthy who prefer fruitful service to empty promotion, the useful declining the throne; the olive's refusal; the fruitful service preferred, vv.8). The verse holds together the olive's refusal.

On "But the olive tree said unto them, Should I leave my fatness," the witnesses note the refusal. Gill notes the olive declining to leave its fatness (the refusal, Psalm 104:15) — the refusal (leave my fatness).

The refusal displays the worthy who prefer fruitful service to empty promotion. The verse displays the olive: content. The verse displays the service: fruitful. The verse displays the promotion: empty. The olive declining to reign displays the worthy who prefer fruitful service to empty promotion: the olive tree said unto them, Should I leave my fatness, wherewith by me they honour God and man, and go to be promoted over the trees? (Psalm 104:15) — the olive refusing the throne, unwilling to forsake its useful fatness that honours God and man for the empty honour of rule. The verse displays the fig tree likewise declining, unwilling to leave its sweetness (vv.10-11).
10:
"And the trees said to the fig tree, Come thou, and reign over us." The very brief verse notes the trees asking the fig to reign. The grammatical voices note the second offer. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the trees said to the fig tree, Come thou, and reign over us" — the trees turned next to the fig tree; the and the trees said to the fig tree, Come thou, and reign over us (the fig asked); the figure (the second worthy tree sought, the fruitful again offered the rule; the truth that the trees, refused by the olive, asked the fig, another useful tree, Judges 9:8; the figure of the worthy again sought and again to decline, the fruitful preferring service; the offer to the fig; the second refusal to come, vv.8). The verse holds together the offer to the fig. The verse displays the parable advancing, the useful trees one by one approached and one by one declining the empty honour of rule. The verse displays the truth that the worthy and fruitful are content in their callings and do not grasp at lordship.

On "And the trees said to the fig tree," the witnesses note the offer. Gill notes the trees turning to the fig (the offer, Judges 9:8) — the offer (to the fig).

On "Come thou, and reign over us," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the rule offered: "Come thou, and reign over us" — the fig asked to reign (the second offer); the figure (the worthy again sought to decline); the providence (the offer, the fig's refusal to follow, vv.11). The verse displays the trees asking the fig to reign.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the offer — "the trees said to the fig tree, Come thou, and reign over us," the offer (Keil, Gill); "And the trees said to the fig tree," the offer (Judges 9:8, Gill); "Come thou, and reign over us," the rule (Ellicott, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the offer. The verse marks the fig: asked. The verse displays the rule: offered. The verse displays the worthy: sought again. The verse displays the fruitful: to decline. The offer displays the worthy again sought and again to decline. The verse displays the trees: seeking. The verse displays the fig: useful. The verse displays the throne: offered. The trees asking the fig to reign display the worthy again sought to decline: the trees said to the fig tree, Come thou, and reign over us (Judges 9:8) — the trees turning next to the fig tree, another useful tree, the worthy again offered the rule they would decline. The verse displays the fig refusing, unwilling to leave its sweetness and good fruit (vv.11).
11:
"But the fig tree said unto them, Should I forsake my sweetness, and my good fruit, and go to be promoted over the trees?" The very brief verse notes the fig declining to reign, unwilling to leave its sweetness. The grammatical voices note the refusal. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the fig tree said unto them, Should I forsake my sweetness, and my good fruit, and go to be promoted over the trees?" — the fig refused, unwilling to leave its sweetness for rule; the but the fig tree said unto them, Should I forsake my sweetness, and my good fruit (the fig's refusal); the and go to be promoted over the trees? (the rule declined); the figure (the fig content with its sweet fruit, declining the empty honour of rule; the truth that the fig, like the olive, would not forsake its good fruit for lordship, Luke 13:6; the figure of the worthy who prefer their fruitful calling to empty promotion; the fig's refusal; the sweet fruit preferred, vv.9). The verse holds together the fig's refusal.

On "But the fig tree said unto them, Should I forsake my sweetness, and my good fruit," the witnesses note the refusal. Gill notes the fig declining to leave its sweetness (the refusal, Luke 13:6) — the refusal (forsake my sweetness).

On "and go to be promoted over the trees?" the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the promotion: "go to be promoted over the trees?" — the empty promotion declined (the rule refused); the figure (the worthy who prefer their fruitful calling); the providence (the refusal, the vine declining likewise to follow, vv.12-13). The verse displays the fig declining to reign.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the refusal — "the fig tree said unto them, Should I forsake my sweetness, and my good fruit... and go to be promoted over the trees?" the refusal (Keil, Gill); "But the fig tree said unto them, Should I forsake my sweetness, and my good fruit," the refusal (Luke 13:6, Gill); "and go to be promoted over the trees?" the promotion (Ellicott, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the refusal. The verse marks the fig: declining. The verse displays the sweetness: useful. The verse displays the fruit: good. The verse displays the rule: refused. The refusal displays the worthy who prefer their fruitful calling to empty promotion. The verse displays the fig: content. The verse displays the fruit: preferred. The verse displays the promotion: empty. The fig declining to reign displays the worthy who prefer their fruitful calling to empty promotion: the fig tree said unto them, Should I forsake my sweetness, and my good fruit, and go to be promoted over the trees? (Luke 13:6) — the fig refusing the throne, unwilling to forsake its sweet fruit for the empty honour of rule, the worthy preferring fruitful service. The verse displays the vine likewise declining, unwilling to leave its wine (vv.12-13).
12:
"Then said the trees unto the vine, Come thou, and reign over us." The very brief verse notes the trees asking the vine to reign. The grammatical voices note the third offer. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the trees said unto the vine, Come thou, and reign over us" — the trees turned next to the vine; the then said the trees unto the vine, Come thou, and reign over us (the vine asked); the figure (the third worthy tree sought, the fruitful again offered the rule; the truth that the trees, refused twice, asked the vine, another useful tree, Judges 9:8; the figure of the worthy again sought and again to decline, the fruitful preferring service; the offer to the vine; the third offer to reign, vv.8). The verse holds together the offer to the vine. The verse displays the parable pressing on, the worthy trees one after another approached and refusing the rule. The verse displays the truth that the useful and fruitful are content in their callings and will not leave them for empty lordship.

On "Then said the trees unto the vine," the witnesses note the offer. Gill notes the trees turning to the vine (the offer, Judges 9:8) — the offer (to the vine).

On "Come thou, and reign over us," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the rule offered: "Come thou, and reign over us" — the vine asked to reign (the third offer); the figure (the worthy again sought to decline); the providence (the offer, the vine's refusal to follow, vv.13). The verse displays the trees asking the vine to reign.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the offer — "the trees said unto the vine, Come thou, and reign over us," the offer (Keil, Gill); "Then said the trees unto the vine," the offer (Judges 9:8, Gill); "Come thou, and reign over us," the rule (Ellicott, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the offer. The verse marks the vine: asked. The verse displays the rule: offered. The verse displays the worthy: sought again. The verse displays the fruitful: to decline. The offer displays the worthy again sought and again to decline. The verse displays the trees: seeking. The verse displays the vine: useful. The verse displays the throne: offered. The trees asking the vine to reign display the worthy again sought to decline: the trees said unto the vine, Come thou, and reign over us (Judges 9:8) — the trees turning next to the vine, another useful tree, the worthy again offered the rule they would decline. The verse displays the vine refusing, unwilling to leave its wine that cheers God and man (vv.13).
13:
"And the vine said unto them, Should I leave my wine, which cheereth God and man, and go to be promoted over the trees?" The witnesses note the vine declining to reign, unwilling to leave its wine that cheers God and man — the crux of the worthy who prize their God-given fruitfulness above the empty honour of rule. On "Should I leave my wine, which cheereth God and man, and go to be promoted over the trees?" interpreters expound the refusal. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the fruitful service preferred — "and the vine said unto them, Should I leave my wine, which cheereth God and man, and go to be promoted over the trees?": the vine, like the olive and fig, refuses the rule, unwilling to forsake its wine; the witnesses note the significance — the third and crowning refusal of the worthy, the vine prizing its God-given fruitfulness, the wine that gladdens God in the drink offering and man in his joy, above the empty and burdensome honour of lordship, the best of the trees content in their useful callings and unwilling to leave them for rule (the wine that cheers God, used in the offerings, and man, in his gladness, the vine's service better than a throne, the worthy declining what the worthless will grasp); the lesson — that the true servant of God prizes his appointed fruitfulness above place and power, content to bless God and man in his calling rather than to be exalted, "not slothful in business; fervent in spirit; serving the Lord," Romans 12:11; the truth that to leave a fruitful service for empty honour is no gain but loss, the useful life better than the lofty seat, "godliness with contentment is great gain," 1 Timothy 6:6; the figure — the wine that cheers God and man a figure of the gladness God's true servants bring, fulfilled in the true Vine whose fruit gladdens God and man, "I am the true vine," John 15:1; the truth that the worthy seek not promotion but fruitfulness, and that the desire to rule is no mark of grace; the figure of the worthy who prize their God-given fruitfulness above the empty honour of rule; the call to be content and fruitful in our callings; the warning that to grasp at rule is to leave the better part. The figure of the worthy who prize their God-given fruitfulness above the empty honour of rule.

On "and the vine said unto them, Should I leave my wine, which cheereth God and man," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the wine that cheers. Keil and Ellicott note the vine unwilling to leave its wine, which gladdens God in the offerings and man in his joy, the fruitful service better than rule (the fruitfulness prized, Romans 12:11) — the refusal (leave my wine).

On "and go to be promoted over the trees?" the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the empty promotion. Calvin and Gill note the vine declining the empty honour of rule, the useful life better than the lofty seat, the worthy prizing fruitfulness above place, a figure of the true Vine whose fruit gladdens God and man (the contentment in service, 1 Timothy 6:6; John 15:1) — the figure (the wine that cheers God and man, the gladness God's servants bring); the providence (the refusal, the bramble accepting the rule to follow, vv.14-15). The verse displays the vine declining to reign, prizing its wine.

The witnesses agree the substance: the vine refuses the rule, unwilling to forsake its wine that cheers God and man (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of fruitfulness prized above rule: should I leave my wine, which cheereth God and man — the third and crowning refusal of the worthy, the vine prizing its God-given fruitfulness, the wine that gladdens God in the offering and man in his joy, above the empty honour of lordship, the true servant prizing his appointed fruitfulness above place and power (Romans 12:11), the useful life better than the lofty seat, godliness with contentment great gain (1 Timothy 6:6), a figure of the true Vine whose fruit gladdens God and man (John 15:1). The verse displays the fruitfulness-prized crux. The verse marks the vine: declining. The verse displays the wine: cheering God and man. The verse displays the fruitfulness: prized. The verse displays the rule: refused. The refusal displays the worthy who prize their God-given fruitfulness above the empty honour of rule. The verse displays the service: better than a throne. The verse displays the true Vine: the gladness of God and man. The verse displays the promotion: empty. The vine declining to reign, prizing its wine, displays the crux: Should I leave my wine, which cheereth God and man, and go to be promoted over the trees? (Romans 12:11; 1 Timothy 6:6; John 15:1) — the worthy who prize their God-given fruitfulness above the empty honour of rule, the wine that cheers God and man better than a throne, a figure of the true Vine. The verse displays the bramble accepting the rule and threatening fire (vv.14-15).
